comment.body:
I could talk about this for hours lol. The Bible is very consistent when it comes to fear. To fear God means to trust God; not to try and avoid punishment. Whether you look at Old Testament or New Testament. God never judges us for our fear; rather He just asks us why we have such little faith? 

Those who do bad in this world whether China or US or any of the world leaders seek control of situations they can’t control or aren’t meant to control rather than have faith in Gods love. The Bible says to love is to know God. It is a gift to us whether we sin or not. When we fall short (like adultery), the guilt eats away at some people which just pushes them closer to repeating the sin. That cycle of self destruction brings the problems we have around the world since many people project their pain onto others rather than face the truth within themselves. It’s a lack of faith. It’s a lack of love. It’s a lack of self reflection and acceptance of God’s love. Some of those people lead governments of the world

comment.body:
Very important question!
the key is, that: there is a collection of counterparties acting independently form each other, and the effect of their net activity aggregates. Different counterparties have different offsetting needs if there is any. But for us, traders, there is no edge on monitoring individual firms separately. 
I know that MMs’ risk exposures are fluid, often adjusted on a second-by-second basis, and so any snapshot is only a temporary state. Their profit comes from managing the bid/ask spread. 
...
The key is, again, that the firms inventory balance nets. 
And all what we are looking at is the net effect. This is all what we are interested in, and what matters from tape-reading perspective.
We are not MMs, or brokers to monitor their activities in every detailes.
SEC, CFTC obligates MM firms to be risk neutral, and to report aggregate datas (that one can buy)..

comment.body:
@marvin romanow  one can simply derive estimated gamma based on the reported volume data from exchanges. it is purchaseable.
the common assumption is that MMs hedge both regular and flex options, while firms only hedge regular ones. 
the difference is that regular options (listed on CBOE) have fixed strikes and expos and contract sizes, while on flex contract the customer can choose these terms. 
I always take both into account.
comment.raw_body:
${0}  one can simply derive estimated gamma based on the reported volume data from exchanges. it is purchaseable.
the common assumption is that MMs hedge both regular and flex options, while firms only hedge regular ones. 
the difference is that regular options (listed on CBOE) have fixed strikes and expos and contract sizes, while on flex contract the customer can choose these terms. 
I always take both into account.

comment.body: So weekly post for NVDA is, lead investors into bear trap up to Wednesday, then surge after earnings into Friday? Or, lead bears into bear trap, NVDA is stable leading up to Wednesday earnings, flush it into Friday earnings expo, and rebound next week/rally into March OPEX? Trying to get a general semblance of the expected price action.
comment.raw_body: So weekly post for NVDA is, lead investors into bear trap up to Wednesday, then surge after earnings into Friday? Or, lead bears into bear trap, NVDA is stable leading up to Wednesday earnings, flush it into Friday earnings expo, and rebound next week/rally into March OPEX? Trying to get a general semblance of the expected price action.

comment.body:
they are... yes... OD worked toghether with ex-CBOE MM to build its model.
i also have MM friends and quants that work for MM desks, ex-class mates, who helped me with it. 
of course, no 100% transparency. 
and also, sudden orders can hit anytime (tail events).
but, these estimations are good enough to work in a significant % of times, and thats what matters. 
..
however, this is one of the main reason I keep emphatizing that DEALER FLOW/GREEKS ARE NOT EVERYTHING.
this is only a fraction of the pic that a trader must see.
Read my educational posts, pls, you will see too what I mean by this. 
i don't like over-emphatizing greeks as it would be the only thing... it is a reflexive consequence. 
A trader who wants to frontrun the market looks at the momentums of the data shifts and think hours about the possible underlying reasons.

comment.body:
Yes that’s true. I should clarify speculators, as investors buy and hold and make up whale positions, keeping the float of shares trading stable. So yes, when all of a sudden shares trading expands from long term investors liquidating, then comes the 20% correction.

So, if fundamental news doesn’t shift investor’s outlook, you would expect speculators to dump post earnings into Friday? And a rebound next week into March OPEX?
